message.content:
You are critically reviewing the design capture for recipe-bottle (RBK) heat ₣BZ
("rbk-14-citizen-model"). Two artifacts: a PADDOCK (shape only) and a MECHANICS MEMO (detail).

The PADDOCK (current committed text) is reproduced verbatim here:
----- PADDOCK BEGIN -----
undefined
----- PADDOCK END -----

The MECHANICS MEMO is the file Memos/memo-20260605-citizen-capability-model.md — READ IT.

Context you must hold:
- "Citizen" is a freshly-minted term for the KEYFILE-tier operator identity (a depot-issued
  service account holding one RBRA key). The future federation tier's identity is a "federate."
  The payor is the founder, outside both tiers.
- The model decouples identity lifecycle from capability lifecycle; capabilities are named
  "capability-sets" (IAM grant bundles in code); intent is stored in a "declared roster";
  an audit diffs declared-vs-IAM.
- The paddock holds SHAPE; the memo holds MECHANISM. Scoping/heat-chopping is INTENTIONALLY
  deferred — do NOT file "decide the scope" as a finding; that is a known open.
- A prior review pass already corrected ~11 issues, and the text above already reflects them.
  Do NOT re-report things already fixed in the current text. Focus on improvements NOT yet
  present. If you believe an already-present claim is wrong, say so explicitly.

Source documents you may cross-check (READ the ones relevant to your lens):
- Memos/memo-20260527-operator-credential-models.md (committed two-tier credential plan; the model claims to supersede it)
- Memos/memo-20260604-credential-churn-leak-and-propagation-races.md (credential lifecycle split — the enabler)
- Memos/memo-20260605-governor-mantle-tombstone-leak.md (standalone governor-mantle tombstone-leak fix)
- Tools/rbk/vov_veiled/RBSHR-HorizonRoadmap.adoc ("Operator federation" + envoy/embassy roadmap entries)
- cult-verb specs under Tools/rbk/vov_veiled/: Tools/rbk/vov_veiled/RBSDK-director_invest.adoc, Tools/rbk/vov_veiled/RBSDD-director_divest.adoc, Tools/rbk/vov_veiled/RBSRK-retriever_invest.adoc, Tools/rbk/vov_veiled/RBSDR-director_roster.adoc, Tools/rbk/vov_veiled/RBSGM-governor_mantle.adoc, Tools/rbk/vov_veiled/RBSRA-CredentialFormat.adoc

Rules: Be a genuine, skeptical critic — only report REAL, substantiated issues, each with a
concrete fix. Cite evidence precisely (a quote, a spec name, a file:area, or a URL). It is fine
to find few issues. Do NOT pad. Prefer high-value findings. Your final output is structured data,
not a human message.

ADVERSARIALLY VERIFY this candidate finding. Independently check its evidence — READ the cited file/spec, or WEB-SEARCH the cited fact; do NOT trust the finding's own evidence text. Default to verdict "false" if you cannot substantiate it. If it is real but the stated fix is wrong or imprecise, mark "partial" and give a corrected refined_fix.

FINDING (JSON):
{"title":"Surplus-via-definition-shrink gives malicious revocation cover, but also can mask malicious *expansion*","location":"memo §The audit (lines 124-130, definition-shrink surplus + capability-set definition change)","type":"security","severity":"medium","issue":"The memo notes the one tooling-side surplus source is a capability-set definition *shrink*, and uses this to justify report-not-revoke for all surplus (line 130). But capability-set definitions are code (line 85), and the audit compares actual IAM to the *current* code definition. An attacker who can land a code change that *expands* a capability-set definition (adds a powerful binding) converts every roster member of that set into an auto-converge deficit on the next audit — the audit will silently grant the new binding to all holders, framed as routine deficit-healing. The memo treats definition change as benign 'deficit-on-additions, auto-heal' (line 131) with 'no special versioning machinery.' That is precisely the danger: a definition expansion is a privilege grant to every holder, applied automatically, with the same logged-but-ungated treatment as a failed grant retry. The trust boundary silently shifts from 'who can setIamPolicy' to 'who can merge a one-line change to the capability-set definitions.'","evidence":"memo line 85 'Capability-set definitions are code'; line 131 'A capability-set definition change decomposes into deficit-on-additions (auto-heal ...) ... no special versioning machinery'; lines 105-107 audit 'auto-converge' deficit. The asymmetry treats all additions as ours-to-heal regardless of whether the addition is a code-review event with security weight.","suggested_fix":"Treat a capability-set *definition expansion* as a privilege change requiring the same gate as a surplus legitimization, not as routine deficit auto-heal. At minimum, require that a definition change which increases bindings be human-acknowledged on first roster-wide apply (a 'definition bump' confirmation), distinct from re-converging a transient grant failure. Note in the memo that 'who can edit the capability-set definitions' is a first-class authority equal to grant authority.","confidence":"medium"}

attachment.content:
- cma-prep-pr: Prepare branch for upstream PR contribution
- rbk-prep-release: Prepare release candidate for upstream delivery
- rbk-wrangle: Muster, then roster RBK and JJK heats as two space-padded plain-text blocks
- vvc-BREAK-LOCK: Force-break a stuck VVC lock after a crash.
- vvc-commit: Guarded git commit
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text: I'll read the mechanics memo and cross-check the cited claims before rendering a verdict.

content.content:
1	# Citizen Tier — Identity / Capability Mechanics
2	
3	Date: 2026-06-05
4	
5	Status: Design captured during the 260605 grooming conversation for heat ₣BZ
6	(`rbk-14-citizen-model`). The *shape* lives in the ₣BZ paddock; this memo holds the
7	*mechanism* the paddock points to. Nothing here is built yet. Amended 260605 after an
8	independent review pass (findings #1–#5, #9, #11, #12).
9	
10	## Scope and vocabulary
11	
12	This heat builds the **citizen tier** — the current, keyfile, no-org operator-credential
13	tier — restructured so its capability layer is identical to the future federation tier's, so
14	that federation plugs in later by swapping identity-kind and token mint and nothing else.
15	
16	- **Citizen** — a keyfile-tier operator identity: a depot-issued service account holding one
17	  RBRA key. Rank-neutral (governor, director, retriever are all citizens); the home of the
18	  identity, not the role. Possession of the key is the identity.
19	- **Federate** (future, not this heat) — a federation-tier operator identity: an external-IdP
20	  `principal://` subject the depot recognizes by treaty. A federate holds no key.
21	- **Payor** — the founding human (OAuth refresh token), outside both tiers; neither citizen nor
22	  federate (memo-20260527).
23	- **Capability-set** — a named bundle of IAM grants, defined in code (the role's binding list,
24	  lifted out of today's invest bodies). Tier-blind.
25	- **Declared roster** — depot-resident data mapping identity → {capability-sets held}.
26	  Tier-blind.
27	- **Holdings** — the depot's artifacts (arks/hoards/bullions). Property, not identities. The
28	  depot's civic ontology — citizens/federates (people) + holdings (property) — covers *operator*
29	  identities; cloud-native system identities (Mason, future Envoy) sit outside it, reusing the
30	  capability-set abstraction without the citizen lifecycle (whether they deserve their own
31	  ontological category is deferred).
32	
33	The convergence: the **capability layer** (capability-sets, grant/revoke, the declared roster,
34	the audit) is tier-blind and shared. The **identity** differs by tier (citizen vs federate) —
35	that difference is the one real seam. "Structural isomorphism" means the capability layer is
36	identical across tiers *modulo the opaque principal handle the identity layer supplies*
37	(`serviceAccount:…` vs `principal://…`); only identity-kind, that handle, and the token mint
38	(signed-JWT vs STS) differ.
39	
40	## Verb dissolution
41	
42	Today's role-keyed verbs decompose into a generic register parameterized by
43	(actor, capability-set, identity):
44	
45	| Today | Decomposes to | Notes |
46	|---|---|---|
47	| invest (director/retriever) | add-citizen + grant(set) | governor is actor |
48	| mantle (governor) | add-citizen + grant(governor-set) | payor is actor; mantle is not special |
49	| divest | revoke(all held) + remove-citizen | |
50	| roster | read the declared roster | |
51	| — | grant(set) / revoke(set) | the capability axis, tier-blind |
52	| — | rekey | keyfile-only identity-backing maintenance |
53	
54	Actor and capability-set are parameters, not verb variants. Federation reuses the capability
55	half (grant/revoke/roster) verbatim and swaps the identity half (add-federate, no rekey).
56	
57	## Identity verbs branch; capability verbs do not
58	
59	- **Capability verbs (grant/revoke)** never branch on tier — both are an IAM binding on a
60	  pre-existing identity.
61	- **Identity verbs** are tier-specific (this is the seam):
62	  - *citizen* (this heat): add = mint SA + key + deliver RBRA; rekey = rotate the key, bindings
63	    untouched; remove = revoke-all then delete the SA.
64	  - *federate* (future): add ≈ first grant (identity owned by the IdP, not minted here); no rekey
65	    (the IdP owns rotation); remove = de-register + revoke-all (the principal is not ours to
66	    delete).
67	  - *payor*: install / refresh a human OAuth refresh token.
68	- Token mint also branches (citizen: signed-JWT; federate: STS) and sits below everything.
69	
70	## The declared roster
71	
72	Why intent must be stored (the retired "derive from IAM" cinch was self-defeating once the role
73	prefix leaves the identity name):
74	
75	1. Without a name prefix, deriving the roster from IAM is lossy reverse-mapping — a director set
76	   is several bindings smeared across project + Mason SA + repo + bucket; partial and ambiguous
77	   matches become representable.
78	2. You cannot audit IAM against itself — "intent = whatever IAM says" makes intended-vs-actual a
79	   tautology that always passes, discarding the drift signal.
80	
81	Federation forces the same roster anyway (a federated principal carries no role marker;
82	memo-20260527: "the depot remains the home of the roster"), so the roster is paid once for both
83	tiers and makes routine roster identical and O(1) across tiers.
84	
85	- **Code vs data.** Capability-set *definitions* are code; the roster is *data* (memberships).
86	  The audit compares: for each (identity, declared-set), are the code-defined bindings present in
87	  IAM?
88	- **Physical home (open).** A GCS object in the depot bucket — mutable, high-churn, etag-guarded
89	  read-modify-write (like IAM policies themselves) — is the natural fit, unlike tripwire-protected
90	  RBRD. A GAR artifact is an alternative. Decide in-heat — but the home must place roster-write
91	  authority at least on par with grant authority (see the audit invariant below), so this is a
92	  security decision, not only storage.
93	- **Writers.** The governor writes director/retriever entries; the payor writes the governor
94	  entry — matching the authority hierarchy.
95	
96	## The audit
97	
98	Drift taxonomy (for one identity; D = union of declared sets' bindings, A = actual RBK-relevant
99	bindings):
100	
101	| State | Meaning | Likely cause | Heal |
102	|---|---|---|---|
103	| D = A | clean | — | — |
104	| deficit-whole | a declared set wholly absent in A | grant never ran / errant revoke | auto-converge |
105	| deficit-fragment | a declared set partly present | half-failed multi-scope grant | auto-converge |
106	| surplus-complete | a whole undeclared named set in A | out-of-band grant | report |
107	| surplus-fragment | bindings belonging to no whole set | tampering / shrunk definition | report |
108	
109	**Asymmetric healing = the Pale recognition test:**
110	
111	- **Deficit is ours** (our own grant failed to complete) → auto-converge up to D (re-run the
112	  idempotent grant), logged, never silent.
113	- **Surplus crossed the Pale** (IAM touched outside our verbs) → characterize, report, let a human
114	  adjudicate (legitimize → declare it, or reject → revoke it). Never auto-revoke (a stale roster
115	  would deprovision a working identity); never auto-backfill intent (re-enters the tautology). No
116	  verb auto-revokes; the destructive operation is always human-driven.
117	
118	**Invariant the asymmetry rests on.** Auto-converging deficits treats the declared roster as
119	authoritative intent, so writing the roster *upward* is itself a grant. The auto-heal is safe only
120	if roster-write authority is at least as protected as the grant verbs (setIamPolicy); otherwise it
121	is a softer escalation path than setIamPolicy. The roster's physical home (above) must satisfy that
122	ACL constraint — it is a security boundary, not a storage convenience.
123	
124	Consequences:
125	
126	- Because grant writes intent *first*, our tooling cannot produce a surplus *absent a definition
127	  change* — so a surplus is normally a clean "someone bypassed the verbs" signal. The one
128	  tooling-side surplus source is a capability-set definition *shrink* (next bullet), which strands a
129	  binding our own earlier grant placed — which is why surplus is reported, not auto-revoked,
130	  regardless of origin.
131	- A capability-set definition change decomposes into deficit-on-additions (auto-heal via
132	  roster-wide re-converge) + surplus-on-removals (report for human-confirmed revoke) — no special
133	  versioning machinery.
134	- Audit domain = roster-identities × set-defined bindings; non-roster principals are out of scope.
135	
136	## Orphan hazard and the identity marker (citizen tier only)
137	
138	With no name prefix and no SA labels, nothing marks an SA as RBK-managed — so a failed add-citizen
139	can strand a live key for an untracked SA (a credential leak you cannot find). Marker options:
140	
141	- **SA tags** (tag bindings): key-value, listable, condition-usable — the proper metadata
142	  mechanism. Caveat: SA tags are **Preview** (may change).
143	- **SA `description` sentinel** (`rbk:…`): stable fallback, used only for the orphan-sweep; the
144	  roster owns capability, the description earns exactly this one job.
145	
146	Federation has no key to leak and a stray principal binding is visible in the policy, so this
147	hazard is citizen-tier-specific.
148	
149	## Authority — who may grant which capability-set
150	
151	- **Resource-scope-split principle.** An authority boundary is IAM-real only where it coincides
152	  with a resource boundary. Payor/governor holds because billing is a separate resource that
153	  governor's `roles/owner` excludes. Governor/director does not — both live in project IAM and
154	  governor has owner over it — so "governor may grant only director/retriever" is, by itself,
155	  honor-system.
156	- **IAM can scope grants by role** (corrects the first paddock draft). The condition attribute
157	  `iam.googleapis.com/modifiedGrantsByRole` limits which roles a setIamPolicy-capable principal
158	  may grant/revoke:
159	  `api.getAttribute('iam.googleapis.com/modifiedGrantsByRole', []).hasOnly(['roles/x','roles/y'])`.
160	  Limits: works only on **project/folder/org** allow policies, **not** resource-level (AR repo,
161	  bucket, individual SA); ≤10 roles per condition; don't join `hasOnly` with `&&`/`||`; some
162	  services don't recognize the limit (request fails); the governor must NOT hold plain
163	  `roles/owner` (unconditional setIamPolicy) — give it a conditioned setIamPolicy instead.
164	  Google's own caveat — don't let a limited admin grant roles bearing setIamPolicy — is satisfied
165	  at *project* scope: the project-scoped director/retriever grants bear no setIamPolicy. The
166	  repo-scoped `roles/artifactregistry.repoAdmin` *does* carry
167	  `artifactregistry.repositories.setIamPolicy` — but that is exactly the resource-scope residual
168	  the detective audit covers, and modifiedGrantsByRole does not reach repo scope anyway.
169	- **Composed resolution.**
170	  - Project-scoped grants → IAM-limit on the governor (preventive, server-side).
171	  - Resource-scoped grants (repo/bucket/SA) → not IAM-limitable → detective audit covers the
172	    residual.
173	  - Topology → pull grant authority to the payor where a standing governor buys nothing.
174	- **Tier-dependent.** Keyfile/solo (payor == admin) → topology: a standing owner-holding citizen
175	  buys nothing and is pure attack surface; drop or demote it. Federation/multi-admin (no
176	  exfiltrable key) → a condition-limited governor is the right preventive tool.
177	- **Audit ↔ topology seam.** Surplus-detection's security value depends on topology: a broad
178	  standing governor makes surplus-detection the only escalation tripwire (detective-only, weak vs
179	  malice); authority at the payor closes escalation structurally and demotes the audit to
180	  honest-mistake hygiene. Do not substitute detective audit for structural closure.
181	- **Ephemeral governor** (decoupling-enabled option): grant the governor-set for one op, then
182	  revoke. Shrinks resting owner-exposure; relevant only if a keyfile delegated governor is ever
183	  wanted.
184	
185	## Blast radius — full reasoning
186	
187	One citizen per person = one SA = one key carrying the **union** of granted capabilities. This is
188	**forced** by "one identity per person": an SA's key always wields the SA's full bindings; you
189	cannot scope one key to a subset. The only escape — separate SAs per role — is the old model,
190	which discards the citizen goal. So accepting multi-role on one citizen *is* accepting the union
191	key; they are one decision.
192	
193	- Cost: larger resting target (a leaked key carries more).
194	- Bounded: the *resting-key* form of this exposure is citizen-tier-only (a file to steal);
195	  federation has no key at rest. The union-of-capabilities-on-one-identity property is itself
196	  tier-blind — a federate granted director+retriever wields both too, exposed through a hijacked
197	  live session rather than a file at rest. Most citizens wear one hat (one-per-person ==
198	  one-per-role).
199	- Upside: finer incident response — revoke a capability without touching the citizen/key, or rekey
200	  without touching capabilities. The old model's only lever was delete-and-recreate.
201	
202	## Governor teardown leak (folds in)
203	
204	`rbgp_governor_mantle` deletes outgoing `governor-*` SAs without first revoking their grants,
205	minting a `deleted:…?uid=` tombstone per re-mantle. The leak closes cheaply: add the generic
206	revoke-before-delete (reusing the rbk-08 revoke layer + Class-C propagation tolerance) before the
207	existing delete — no tombstone, no naming change. The further idempotent-governor refinement (rekey
208	instead of delete+recreate, dropping the delete entirely) is *not* free: it needs a *stable*
209	governor name in place of today's datestamped `governor-YYYYMMDDHHmm` (which exists precisely to
210	dodge the delete→recreate flap), plus a one-time migration of existing datestamped governors.
211	Standalone leak fix (if the heat descopes the governor):
212	memo-20260605-governor-mantle-tombstone-leak.md.
213	
214	## Verified facts (checked 260605)
215	
216	- **Service accounts do not support resource labels.** They support **tags** (tag bindings),
217	  currently in Preview. So the orphan marker uses tags (Preview) or the description sentinel, not a
218	  label.
219	- **IAM can limit which roles a principal may grant** via `modifiedGrantsByRole`, but only on
220	  project/folder/org policies and ≤10 roles — not on resource-level policies.
221	
222	Sources:
223	- Set limits on granting roles — https://docs.cloud.google.com/iam/docs/setting-limits-on-granting-roles
224	- Tags for service accounts — https://docs.cloud.google.com/iam/docs/service-accounts-tags
225	- Service accounts overview — https://docs.cloud.google.com/iam/docs/service-account-overview
226	
227	## memo-20260527 divergence (record when the model is frozen)
228	
229	memo-20260527 states the operator verbs "keep their names, only their bodies differ," and that
230	keyfile holds "one SA key file each per role." This heat supersedes both: the verb *bodies*
231	converge in shape (not just names), and the citizen tier is one SA *per person*, not per role.
232	
233	A third divergence the paddock leans on by name: 20260527's "single code seam" is a *role-keyed*
234	token accessor ("a token as director"), shippable with no behavior change over today's per-role
235	SAs. Under one-SA-per-person the accessor becomes *identity-keyed* ("a token as alice", carrying the
236	union) — a generalization of that seam, but *not* itself no-behavior-change, since it presupposes
237	the per-person migration. (Whether the heat's "load-bearing core" is the role-keyed no-behavior
238	stepping-stone or the identity-keyed end state is an open scoping question — see the paddock.)
239	
240	Update memo-20260527 to record these when the model is frozen.
241

content.text:
Now let me carefully examine the relevant section (lines 124-134) and the cited lines for the finding. Let me re-read the key passages closely.

The finding claims:
1. Line 130: surplus is reported, not auto-revoked, "regardless of origin"
2. Line 131: a capability-set definition change decomposes into deficit-on-additions (auto-heal) + surplus-on-removals (report)
3. The danger: a definition *expansion* (adding a binding) becomes an auto-converged deficit for all roster holders — silently granting new privilege to everyone.

Let me verify the exact text and reasoning. The memo lines 126-133 say exactly what the finding quotes. Let me check the audit invariant section (lines 118-122) which the finding doesn't fully engage with, and the authority section.

The key question for adversarial verification: Is the asymmetry genuinely a real, novel danger the memo fails to address? Let me check whether the memo's existing invariant (lines 118-122) already covers this.

The invariant says: "Auto-converging deficits treats the declared roster as authoritative intent, so writing the roster *upward* is itself a grant. The auto-heal is safe only if roster-write authority is at least as protected as the grant verbs (setIamPolicy)."

This invariant addresses *roster* writes (data: who holds which set). It does NOT address *definition* changes (code: what bindings a set contains). The finding's point is precisely that the definition is code, and a code change that expands a set's bindings auto-grants to all holders. This is a distinct attack surface from the roster-write authority the invariant guards.

Let me verify the distinction holds by re-reading lines 85 and 118-122.

content.content:
84	
85	- **Code vs data.** Capability-set *definitions* are code; the roster is *data* (memberships).
86	  The audit compares: for each (identity, declared-set), are the code-defined bindings present in
87	  IAM?
88	- **Physical home (open).** A GCS object in the depot bucket — mutable, high-churn, etag-guarded
89	  read-modify-write (like IAM policies themselves) — is the natural fit, unlike tripwire-protected
90	  RBRD. A GAR artifact is an alternative. Decide in-heat — but the home must place roster-write
91	  authority at least on par with grant authority (see the audit invariant below), so this is a
92	  security decision, not only storage.
93	- **Writers.** The governor writes director/retriever entries; the payor writes the governor
94	  entry — matching the authority hierarchy.
95	
96	## The audit
97	
98	Drift taxonomy (for one identity; D = union of declared sets' bindings, A = actual RBK-relevant
99	bindings):
100	
101	| State | Meaning | Likely cause | Heal |
102	|---|---|---|---|
103	| D = A | clean | — | — |
104	| deficit-whole | a declared set wholly absent in A | grant never ran / errant revoke | auto-converge |
105	| deficit-fragment | a declared set partly present | half-failed multi-scope grant | auto-converge |
106	| surplus-complete | a whole undeclared named set in A | out-of-band grant | report |
107	| surplus-fragment | bindings belonging to no whole set | tampering / shrunk definition | report |
108	
109	**Asymmetric healing = the Pale recognition test:**
110	
111	- **Deficit is ours** (our own grant failed to complete) → auto-converge up to D (re-run the
112	  idempotent grant), logged, never silent.
113	- **Surplus crossed the Pale** (IAM touched outside our verbs) → characterize, report, let a human
114	  adjudicate (legitimize → declare it, or reject → revoke it). Never auto-revoke (a stale roster
115	  would deprovision a working identity); never auto-backfill intent (re-enters the tautology). No
116	  verb auto-revokes; the destructive operation is always human-driven.
117	
118	**Invariant the asymmetry rests on.** Auto-converging deficits treats the declared roster as
119	authoritative intent, so writing the roster *upward* is itself a grant. The auto-heal is safe only
120	if roster-write authority is at least as protected as the grant verbs (setIamPolicy); otherwise it
121	is a softer escalation path than setIamPolicy. The roster's physical home (above) must satisfy that
122	ACL constraint — it is a security boundary, not a storage convenience.
123	
124	Consequences:
125	
126	- Because grant writes intent *first*, our tooling cannot produce a surplus *absent a definition
127	  change* — so a surplus is normally a clean "someone bypassed the verbs" signal. The one
128	  tooling-side surplus source is a capability-set definition *shrink* (next bullet), which strands a
129	  binding our own earlier grant placed — which is why surplus is reported, not auto-revoked,
130	  regardless of origin.
131	- A capability-set definition change decomposes into deficit-on-additions (auto-heal via
132	  roster-wide re-converge) + surplus-on-removals (report for human-confirmed revoke) — no special
133	  versioning machinery.

content.text:
Now I have the precise text. Let me reason through the finding adversarially.

**The finding's core claim:** A capability-set definition is *code* (line 85). The audit compares actual IAM to the *current* code definition (line 86-87). Line 131 says a definition change "decomposes into deficit-on-additions (auto-heal via roster-wide re-converge)." So if an attacker lands a code change that *adds* a binding to a capability-set definition, every roster holder of that set shows a deficit, and the audit auto-converges — silently granting the new (possibly powerful) binding to all holders. The memo frames this as benign routine deficit-healing.

**Verifying against the memo's existing defenses:**

The invariant at lines 118-122 explicitly guards *roster-write authority* (the data: memberships). It does NOT guard *definition-write authority* (the code: what bindings each set contains). The finding's attack vector is the definition (code), which the memo treats as plain code with no special protection — line 132 explicitly says "no special versioning machinery."

So there's a genuine asymmetry in the memo's own reasoning: it carefully argues (lines 118-122) that writing the roster upward is "itself a grant" requiring grant-equal authority. But it does NOT make the parallel argument that *expanding a capability-set definition* is also "itself a grant" applied to every holder. Line 131 explicitly assigns the benign "auto-heal" treatment to definition additions. This is a real gap.

**Is the danger overstated?** Let me steelman the memo's position. Capability-set definitions are code — landing a malicious code change presumably requires code review / merge access, which is a different (and arguably stronger) trust boundary than setIamPolicy. The memo could argue "if you can merge arbitrary code, you already own the system." That's a fair counter. But:

1. The memo *itself* applies exactly this rigor to the roster (data, lower trust) but not to definitions (code) — an inconsistency.
2. The auto-converge means the expansion is applied *automatically on next audit* with no human confirmation gate, framed identically to a failed-grant retry. Even granting that code-merge is a real boundary, the auto-application without distinction blurs a routine-hygiene operation with a privilege-expansion event. The finding's point that "the trust boundary silently shifts to who-can-merge-the-definitions" is substantively correct and not acknowledged in the memo.

The finding correctly notes the memo elsewhere is *very* careful about this exact class of reasoning (the Pale, the invariant). So failing to extend it to definition-expansion is a genuine inconsistency, not just a theoretical nitpick.

**Checking severity.** The mitigating factor (code merge is a stronger gate) makes this somewhat lower than a roster-data attack. But this is a *design memo* being captured; the value of flagging is to add the missing distinction before the heat builds it. The memo explicitly says "no special versioning machinery" — so the gap is concrete and load-bearing for the future implementation. Medium severity is defensible; I'd lean medium given it's design-stage and the gate is code-review-protected.

**Checking the fix.** The finding's suggested fix is reasonable but I'll refine it. Requiring a "definition bump confirmation" on first roster-wide apply is one mechanism. More precisely aligned with the memo's own vocabulary: the memo should extend its invariant (lines 118-122) to cover definition-*expansion* as a grant event, and the audit's auto-converge should distinguish "deficit from a failed grant of an *unchanged* definition" (safe auto-heal) from "deficit newly created by a definition expansion" (a privilege-grant event warranting the same human adjudication as surplus-legitimization). The memo can detect the difference: a definition expansion produces a deficit across *all* holders of the set simultaneously (a roster-wide deficit that did not exist on the prior audit), whereas a failed grant is per-identity. That detection heuristic strengthens the fix beyond "human-acknowledge."

Let me also verify the finding isn't already-fixed. The memo header says it incorporates findings #1-#5, #9, #11, #12. The current text at line 131-132 still says "auto-heal" and "no special versioning machinery" with no gate on expansion — so this concern is NOT yet addressed. It's a genuine not-yet-present improvement.

This is a real finding. Let me render the verdict.

input.reasoning: I read the memo's audit section (lines 96-134) and the cited lines directly rather than trusting the finding's quotes; they check out verbatim. Line 85: "Capability-set definitions are code." Lines 86-87: the audit compares actual IAM to the code-defined bindings of the current definition. Line 131: "A capability-set definition change decomposes into deficit-on-additions (auto-heal via roster-wide re-converge) ... no special versioning machinery." So a code change that *expands* a set's binding list does produce a roster-wide deficit that the audit auto-converges — silently granting the new binding to every holder of that set, with the same logged-not-gated treatment as a transient failed-grant retry. The finding's mechanism is sound. The decisive evidence is the memo's own internal inconsistency: at lines 118-122 it argues carefully that writing the roster *upward* is "itself a grant" and therefore roster-write authority must be at least as protected as setIamPolicy (a real security boundary). It pointedly does NOT extend that identical reasoning to definition expansion — even though a definition expansion is, by the very same logic, a grant applied automatically to every holder. The memo guards the data path (roster) but leaves the code path (definitions) explicitly ungated ("no special versioning machinery"), so the privilege-expansion trust boundary silently relocates from "who can setIamPolicy / write the roster" to "who can merge a one-line change to the capability-set definitions" — a shift the memo never names. This is not already-fixed: the header lists incorporated findings #1-5/#9/#11/#12, and lines 131-132 still carry the unqualified "auto-heal" / "no special versioning machinery" language. The one mitigation the memo could lean on — that landing a code change requires merge/review access, a stronger gate than setIamPolicy — is real and is why I rate this medium rather than high; but it is exactly the distinction the memo fails to draw, and a design-capture memo that is otherwise this rigorous about the Pale should draw it before the heat builds the auto-converge.
input.refined_fix: Extend the audit invariant (memo lines 118-122) and the consequence at line 131 to treat a capability-set definition *expansion* as a grant event, not routine deficit auto-heal. Concretely: (1) State that "who may edit capability-set definitions" is a first-class authority equal to grant/setIamPolicy authority, parallel to the roster-write-authority claim already made — a definition expansion grants new privilege to every holder of that set. (2) Make the audit distinguish two deficit sources rather than auto-converging all deficits identically: a per-identity deficit against an *unchanged* definition (a genuine failed/partial grant — safe to auto-heal, the original case) vs. a deficit that newly appears across *all* holders of a set simultaneously (the signature of a definition expansion). The memo can detect this cheaply — a definition bump shows as a synchronized roster-wide deficit absent on the prior audit, whereas a failed grant is isolated to one identity. (3) Gate the roster-wide case behind the same human adjudication the memo already requires for surplus-legitimization (lines 113-116), e.g. a one-time "definition expansion confirmation" on first apply, distinct from re-converging a transient grant failure. This keeps the memo's "no special versioning machinery" goal for the common case (unchanged-definition deficits still auto-heal) while closing the silent-privilege-grant path for the expansion case. The finding's proposed fix is correct in spirit; this refinement adds the concrete detection heuristic (synchronized roster-wide deficit) so the gate can be triggered automatically rather than relying on an operator to notice the definition changed.

content.text:
I've completed the adversarial verification. The finding is real and substantiated.

The key evidence (verified by reading `Memos/memo-20260605-citizen-capability-model.md` directly):

- Line 85: "Capability-set definitions are code"
- Lines 86-87: the audit compares actual IAM against the *current* code-defined bindings
- Line 131: a definition change "decomposes into deficit-on-additions (auto-heal via roster-wide re-converge) ... no special versioning machinery"

The decisive point is the memo's own internal inconsistency: at lines 118-122 it argues rigorously that writing the roster *upward* is "itself a grant" requiring grant-equal authority — but it never extends that identical Pale-reasoning to a capability-set *definition expansion*, which by the same logic is a grant applied automatically to every holder of the set. The memo guards the data path (roster) and explicitly leaves the code path (definitions) ungated, so the privilege-expansion trust boundary silently relocates to "who can merge a change to the definitions."

I rated it medium (not high) because landing a code change requires merge/review access — a genuinely stronger gate than setIamPolicy — which the memo could lean on, but which is exactly the distinction it fails to draw. I marked the verdict "real" but refined the fix to add a concrete auto-detection heuristic (a definition expansion shows as a synchronized roster-wide deficit absent on the prior audit, vs. a per-identity deficit for a true failed grant), so the gate can fire automatically while preserving the memo's "no special versioning machinery" goal for the common case.
